summary: Kernel is memory-bound with poor memory access efficiency due to non-coalesced loads and high L2 cache miss rate
reasoning: The roofline analysis confirms memory bottleneck with 99.1% Memory SOL vs 76.6% Compute SOL. DRAM throughput at 65.36% of peak with L1 achieving 99.23% throughput indicates memory subsystem saturation. The long scoreboard stalls at 58.52% show warps waiting on memory operations, while only 23.64% of bytes per sector for global loads indicates severe memory access inefficiency.

root_causes.cause: Non-coalesced global memory access pattern due to 3D pooling window traversal causing scattered reads across input tensor

evidence.interpretation: Only 23.64% of loaded bytes are actually used, meaning ~76% of memory bandwidth is wasted on unused data in each 32-byte sector
58.52% of warp stalls are due to waiting for memory operations, confirming memory latency is the primary stall reason

root_causes.cause: High L2 cache miss rate causing excessive DRAM traffic for the 3D sliding window access pattern

evidence.interpretation: L2 cache hit rate of only 42.5% means majority of accesses go to DRAM, increasing memory pressure
While L1 has good hit rate at 84%, the L2 misses still cause significant DRAM traffic at 4.16GB read vs 0.49GB write

recommended_fixes.fix: Restructure the kernel to process output elements in tiles that maximize spatial locality - group consecutive output positions that share input data in the pooling window, and use shared memory to cache input tiles before computing max values across multiple output elements
recommended_fixes.rationale: This would improve memory coalescing by loading contiguous input regions into shared memory once and reusing them for multiple outputs, reducing redundant DRAM accesses and improving the bytes-per-sector efficiency from 23.64% closer to 100%
